# Challenges in recycling

![recycling bin overflowing with trash](/ecenter/sites/default/files/styles/large_image_style/public/article-image/recycling_bin_overflowing_with_trash.jpg?itok=OcLg0lwA)

Recycling seems like an obvious solution to the plastic waste issue plaguing our oceans, air and soil. However, there are many challenges faced by the recycling industry that individual consumers can alleviate.

## [Poor recycling quality due to lack of education](https://www.todayonline.com/voices/step-up-public-education-recycling-tackle-lack-awareness)

Most people know that recycling is a good habit for the environment, but are often unsure of what goes into the recycling bin. This leads to:

## [Safety for workers](https://bergmill.com/2017/06/13/top-5-health-safety-risks-faced-recycling-workers/)

Working in the waste/recycling industry comes with many safety risks including:

These risks turn many workers away from the industry.

## [Expensive or inadequate services](https://www.nytimes.com/2015/11/29/business/energy-environment/big-companies-put-their-money-where-the-trash-is.html)

In order for a city to have an effective recycling system, adequate services (like separate bins and safe recycling centers) are needed. Some cities just don’t have the resources or the government support to provide adequate recycling services.

## [Low market demand for recycled materials](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20200220005559/en/Global-Recycled-Plastics-Market-2020-2024-Rising-Demand-for-Recycled-Plastics-from-the-Packaging-Industry-to-Boost-Market-Growth-Technavio)

For businesses, buying recycled materials is like buying second-hand at full price. Especially during the COVID-induced decrease in oil prices, the price of “virgin” plastics is much lower than the price of post-consumer plastics.

However, the industry is seeing a rebound in the value for recycled content in production and packaging. With supply chain shortages, virgin materials are harder and more costly to acquire. Multiple global economic factors are supporting a rise in demand for recycled feed stock, leading to a larger need to capture quality materials and put them back into the circular economy.

## [Is recycling worth it?](https://www.colorado.edu/ecenter/zero-waste/recycle/materials-details)

**YES...it is!**

Recycling plays a large role in solving the plastic issue. These challenges with recycling can be alleviated if we buy products that are made of recycled materials, educate ourselves on what goes into the recycling bin and go the extra mile to make sure that recyclable materials aren't going to the landfill.

#### Breadcrumb

# Challenges

Organizations face many challenges when managing plastic waste. We describe several of those challenges below.

**On this page:**

## Weak Strategic Planning

![This is a drawing representing a checklist with the words weak strategic planning under it](/system/files/styles/thumbnail/private/images/2024-03/planningchallengesicon.png?itok=-IFLlHYK)

A strategic plan for a solid waste management program outlines the strategy and goals of the program, identifying key projects, policies, costs, and the timeline necessary to reach such goals. A weak strategic plan can limit the effectiveness of solid waste management efforts. For example, weak strategic planning may result in a lack of skilled managers and operators necessary to perform tasks in the solid waste management system.

## Lack of Solid Waste Management Systems and Infrastructure

![This is a drawing of a trash can](/system/files/styles/thumbnail/private/images/2024-03/systemschallengesicon2.png?itok=MezZ9nk5)

Infrastructure for solid waste management has not kept up with the growing manufacturing and usage of single-use plastic products. Localities may struggle to manage plastic waste due to limited availability of waste collection, treatment, and disposal options. They may also lack effective reuse (including refill) systems, which, when in place, can drastically reduce the disposal of plastic products.

## High Cost of Collection and Transportation

![This is a drawing of a dump truck](/system/files/styles/small/private/images/2024-03/collectionchallengesicon2.png?itok=sQLI8Prx)

The high cost of transportation can present a barrier for some solid waste management providers as well as customers. [According to a 2022 study,](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/epub/10.1177/0169796X221104855) this barrier is even more prevalent in small jurisdictions where recycling rates range from zero to 5%. In some places, waste collection services are privately owned utilities with high operational costs and service fees. To avoid paying expensive fees, some residents opt to illegally dump or burn their waste instead.

## Shortage of Funding

![This is a drawing of several bills](/system/files/styles/thumbnail/private/images/2024-03/fundingchallengesicon2.png?itok=hqQHv781)

Lack of funding can pose a key obstacle for localities that wish to purchase or maintain large-scale, locally appropriate technology that can prevent or capture plastics from entering waterways. For more information, refer to [the Recycling Markets companion chapter (pdf)](/system/files/documents/2023-07/SWM_RecyclingMarkets-Final.pdf)(29.5 MB) to the Best Practices for Solid Waste Management: A Guide for Decision-Makers in Developing Countries. EPA is working to combat funding shortages through grants from [the Solid Waste Infrastructure for Recycling Grant Program.](/infrastructure/solid-waste-infrastructure-recycling-grant-program)

## **Lack of Data and Research**

enriched_points.expanded_content: Many recycling systems lack infrastructure and efficient processes, resulting in low participation rates and contamination, ultimately decreasing the quality of recycled materials.
The increasing production of plastic waste has led to overflowing landfills, causing significant environmental hazards and contributing to the depletion of usable land.
Improper disposal of plastic waste leads to pollution of air, soil, and water, posing serious health risks to communities and harming ecosystems.
